coredata.dc:title: Ultrahigh voltage window, preeminent energy density aqueous supercapacitor derived from honeycomb-like porous carbon decorated with carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Xin
Ren, Penggang
Dai, Zhong
Chen, Hongtao
Tang, Wenwen
Chen, Zhengyan
Ren, Fang
Jin, Yanling
coredata.dc:description: The development of advanced electrode materials with high specific capacitance and wide voltage window is of great importance in improving the low energy density of aqueous supercapacitors (ASCs). Herein, a novel heteroatoms doped 3 D porous carbon decorated with 0 D carbon dots is prepared from egg whites and yolks. The unique honeycomb-like interconnected pore structure and multi-component feature endows the prepared material with specific surface area (1999.3 m2/g), high pore volume (1.22 cm3/g) with abundant micropores (0.67 cm3/g) and multi-doped heteroatoms including P, S, O and N, leading to excellent specific capacitance of 472.9 F/g at 1 A/g. More importantly, the symmetric supercapacitor assembled from the prepared porous carbon exhibits the voltage window as high as 2.6 V in 1.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. Hence, the symmetric supercapacitor achieves a leap forward in energy density (107.5 Wh/kg at a power density of 650 W/kg and maintains about 65.2 Wh/kg at 26,000 W/kg) relative to other ASCs. These preeminent results provide important insight for the design of aqueous symmetric carbon-based supercapacitor with ultrahigh voltage window and energy density.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aqueous supercapacitors
Porous carbon
Carbon dots
Voltage window
Energy density

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: . Synthesis of YCDs/ egg-whites-based porous carbon composites (DAEC)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51773167 , 52102303 , 52103095 ), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2019M650268 ), the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering (Sichuan University) (Grant No. sklpme2021-05-09 ), and Foundation of Education Department of Shaanxi Province [ 20JK0805 ], the authors would like to thank Zhang Yuyao from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the Raman analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure of egg-based porous carbon decorated by YCDs.

(a) Photographic images of YCDs suspension in visible light and under UV-light irradiation at 365 nm. (b) FTIR spectra of YCDs, AEC, DAEC. (c) Low magnification TEM image of DAEC. (d) HRTEM image of DAEC.
SEM images of (a) EC, (b) AEC, (c) DEC, (d) DAEC; High magnification of (e) EC, (f) AEC, (g) DEC, (h) DAEC.
(a) XPS spectrum of DAEC, High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s, (e) S2p, (f) P2p.
(a) XRD pattern of EC, AEC and DAEC, (b) Raman spectra of EC, AEC and DAEC, (c) Nitrogen sorption isotherms and (d) pore size distribution of EC, AEC and DAEC.

(a) CV curves of all samples at 100 mV/s, (b) CV curves of DAEC at different scan rates, (c) GCD curves of all samples at 1 A/g, (d) GCD curves of DAEC at various current densities, (e) specific capacitance at various current densities from 0.5 to 20 A/g, (f) Log i vs Log v for all samples, (g) Percentage of capacitance contribution of DAEC at different scan rates, (h) Nyquist plots of all samples with an enlargement of the high frequency region as the inset, (i) Bode phase plots of all samples.

Electrochemical properties of DAEC//DAEC in two-electrode system: In 6 M KOH electrolyte, (a) CV curves with voltage window from 0.6 V to 2.0 V at 50 mV/s, (b) CV curves at 10 mV/s to 200 mV/s, (c) GCD curves (under 1.8 V) from 0.5 A/g to 20 A/g; In 1.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte, (d) CV curves with voltage window from 1 V to 2.8 V at 50 mV/s, (e) CV curves at 10 mV/s to 200 mV/s, (f) GCD curves (under 2.6 V) from 0.5 A/g to 20 A/g, (g) Nyquist plots of DAEC//DAEC in different electrolyte, (h) Ragone plots of DAEC//DAEC and previously reported devices, (i) Cycling stability after 5000 cycles (the inset shows a photograph of two DAEC//DAEC connected in series to power the LEDs).
Textural properties of EC, AEC, DEC and DAEC.
Electrochemical performance of DAEC//DAEC and previously reported devices supercapacitors.

row.entry: Citric acid and urea derived carbon [24]
Hexagon-like P doped Ni(OH)2 rod arrays [38]

head.ce:title: Ultrahigh voltage window, preeminent energy density aqueous supercapacitor derived from honeycomb-like porous carbon decorated with carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Printing, Packaging Engineering and Digital media Technology, Xi'an University of Technology, Jinhua South Road No. 5, Xi'an, Shaanxi 710048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Packaging Engineering and Digital media Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aFaculty of Printing, Packaging Engineering and Digital media Technology, Xi'an University of Technology, Jinhua South Road No. 5, Xi'an, Shaanxi 710048, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xi'an University of Technology, Jinhua South Road No. 5, Xi'an 710048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Materials Science and Engineering, Xi'an University of Technology, Jinhua South Road No. 5, Xi'an 710048, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Faculty of Printing, Packaging Engineering and Digital media Technology, Xi'an University of Technology, Jinhua South Road No. 5, Xi'an, Shaanxi 710048, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Packaging Engineering and Digital media Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of advanced electrode materials with high specific capacitance and wide voltage window is of great importance in improving the low energy density of aqueous supercapacitors (ASCs). Herein, a novel heteroatoms doped 3 D porous carbon decorated with 0 D carbon dots is prepared from egg whites and yolks. The unique honeycomb-like interconnected pore structure and multi-component feature endows the prepared material with specific surface area (1999.3 m2/g), high pore volume (1.22 cm3/g) with abundant micropores (0.67 cm3/g) and multi-doped heteroatoms including P, S, O and N, leading to excellent specific capacitance of 472.9 F/g at 1 A/g. More importantly, the symmetric supercapacitor assembled from the prepared porous carbon exhibits the voltage window as high as 2.6 V in 1.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. Hence, the symmetric supercapacitor achieves a leap forward in energy density (107.5 Wh/kg at a power density of 650 W/kg and maintains about 65.2 Wh/kg at 26,000 W/kg) relative to other ASCs. These preeminent results provide important insight for the design of aqueous symmetric carbon-based supercapacitor with ultrahigh voltage window and energy density.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid advance in electronics technology is driving an increasing demand for high performance energy storage. Aqueous supercapacitors (ASCs) have emerged as one of the most hopeful candidates due to their high power density, splendid cycling durability, superfast charge/discharge rates, environmental-friendliness and outstanding ionic conductivity of aqueous electrolyte [1–3]. Whereas, the further development of ASCs is seriously restricted by their relatively low energy density (E). Based on the equation of E = 1/2 CV2, it is evident that boosting the specific capacitance (C) or/and broadening the voltage window (V) are effective strategies to enhance the energy density. The characteristics of the electrode material play a crucial role in the capacitance. Moreover, the voltage window of ASCs is kept around 1.23 V in virtue of the hydrolysis of the aqueous electrolyte caused by the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at the negative electrode and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) at the positive electrode [4]. Decreasing the activity of HER and OER via optimizing the electrode material is an effective strategy to break through the voltage window. Hence, the selection and optimization of electrode materials is crucial for the preparation of ASCs with high energy density.

Carbon materials are considered as the most widely used electrode materials for ASCs owing to their admirable properties of large specific surface area, adjustable pore structure, wide source and excellent chemical stability. Noteworthily, porous structure engineering is pivotal to the capacitance properties of carbon materials since macropores act as electrolyte storage reservoirs, mesopores afford avenues for fast ions transportation and micropores provide abundant ions adsorption sites. Besides, micropores can also reversibly adsorb native hydrogen and inhibit hydrogen precipitation, thus gratefully broadening the voltage window of ASCs [5,6]. Zhao et al. [7] reported that the porous carbon materials derived from wilted roses exhibited an extended voltage window of 2.1 V due to the existence of abundant micropores. Furthermore, extensive studies have shown that heteroatoms doping including N, O and S can increase the capacitance of porous carbon in virtue of the pseudocapacitance of surface functional groups [8,9]. In addition to contributing pseudocapacitance, P doping also significantly hinders HER by increasing the active sites of adsorbed cations, and enhance the oxidative stability by blocking the active oxidation sites at positive potentials, thereby widening the voltage window [10–12]. Cui et al. [13] developed multi-heteroatom-doped carbon electrodes and confirmed the positive effect of P doping. On this basis, constructing porous carbon with abundant micropores and heteroatoms doping (especially containing P) can markedly boost the specific capacitance and simultaneously widen the voltage window [14–16].

Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely investigated for energy storage and conversion because of their multiple charge trapping centers, high surface area and abundant functional groups on surface. In addition, some unique attributes make the CDs well suite as electroactive components in porous carbon [12,17,18]. Firstly, the adequate dispersion of smaller size CDs in porous carbon can fully increase the microporous content and specific surface area [19,20]. Secondly, CDs contains abundant heteroatoms and functional groups derived from the precursors [21,22]. Thirdly, the strong affinity of CDs for Li+ and Na+ can achieve inhibition of HER, leading to a wide voltage window [23]. Hence, the design of porous carbon with CDs decoration using the advantageous properties of different compositions can significantly improve the specific capacitance and voltage window.

Following the above considerations, natural biomasses are viewed as ideal precursors to achieve the above-mentioned purposes. The richness of protein, cholesterol and lecithin in eggs leads to an intrinsically homogeneous doping of heteroatoms. Porous carbon materials can be ingeniously constructed by mechanical stirring of egg whites, while CDs can be easily prepared by hydrothermal treatment of yolks. The prepared electrode materials exhibit a honeycomb-like interconnected pore structure, a prominent specific surface area of 1999.3 m2/g, a pore volume of 1.22 cm3/g with a large percentage of micropores, and multi-heteroatoms doping, such as N, O, S and P. Such novel structure endows the prepared DAEC electrode with a high specific capacitance of 472.9 F/g at 1 A/g and superb rate performance in 6 M KOH electrolyte. Conspicuously, the DAEC-based symmetric supercapacitor using 1.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte displays an extended voltage window of 2.6 V, optimal energy density of 107.5 Wh/kg at a power density of 650 W/kg and high cycling reversibility. This strategy provides a promising way for designing and engineering carbon-based ASCs with broadened voltage range and boosted energy density.

The eggs were purchased from the local supermarket (Xi'an, China). KOH, urea, polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG 200), acetylene black and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) were provided by Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China).

The yolks separated from egg whites was cooked and crushed into a fine powder, then dried at 80 °C for 12 h. Dried yolks (0.4 g), urea (0.5 g) and deionized water (40 mL) were then placed in an 80 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave reactor under 180 °C for 28 h to complete the hydrothermal reaction. Until natural cooling to ambient temperature, the resulting product was filtered to obtain the supernatant. Subsequently, supernatant (25 mL), PEG 200 (10 mL) and deionized water (5 mL) were mixed in the autoclave and reacted at 220 °C for 12 h. The product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. Finally, the collected supernatant was freeze-dried for 36 h to obtain YCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: . Synthesis of YCDs/ egg-whites-based porous carbon composites (DAEC)
ce:section.ce:para: The YCDs and egg whites mixture with 1:50 mass ratio was continuously whipped until uniform foams appeared and then freeze-dried for 48 h. After that, the resultant powder was heated to 250 °C in muffle furnace at a heating rate of 1 °C/min and held for 60 min to obtain stabilized carbon foam. Then the carbon foam was calcined at 1000 °C for 1 h in N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 5 °C/min. The obtained carbon material was ground into a powder with KOH at a mass ratio of 1:4 and carbonized at 800 °C for 1 h in N2 atmosphere with 10 °C/min. Eventually, the resulting powder was washed to neutral with deionized water and dried at 60 ℃ for 24 h to obtain DAEC. For comparison, pure egg whites derived carbon only with KOH (AEC), pure egg whites derived carbon only with YCDs (DEC) and without any other reagents (EC) were prepared under the same conditions. The schematic of the conversion from eggs to porous carbon is shown in Fig. 1 .

Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7100F) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos-F200S) were used to study the microscopic morphology of the obtained materials. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5) were using circular pressed disks of carbon material/KBr mixture. Measured at X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker AXS D8, Cu Kα) from 10° to 60° to obtain X-ray diffraction patterns. Raman scattering spectra were determined by a Raman spectrometer (HORIBA Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution) with the excitation beam wavelength of 532 nm. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller specific surface area (SBET) and pore size distribution (Barrett-Joyner-Halenda model, BJH) were analyzed from nitrogen adsorption-desorption experiment in specific surface area and porosity analyzer (Quantachrome Autosorb-IQ). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) was used to investigate the surface chemical bonding states and elemental composition.

The working electrode was made from uniform slurry by blending the obtained-carbon materials, acetylene black and PTFE at the mass ratio of 16: 3: 1 with alcohol as dispersant. Subsequently, the obtained slurry was brushed on nickel foams (10×10×1 mm3) and dried at 80 °C for 12 h. By weighing the mass difference before and after loading of nickel foam, the loading mass was calculated to be about 2 mg.

In three electrode system, 6 M KOH aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte. The reference and counter electrode were charged with Hg/HgO and Pt foil (1×1 cm2), respectively. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement were tested on an electrochemical workstation (CS350H, Wuhan) at room temperature.

The specific capacitance (C, F/g) is calculated by the Eq. (1).
(1)
C = I
Δ
t / m
Δ
V
Where I (A) represent the discharge current, Δt (s) is the discharge time, m (g) refers to the weight of obtained-carbon materials on electrode, and ΔV (V) is the discharge voltage.

In two-electrode system, symmetric supercapacitors were assembled by employing two DAEC electrodes with identical mass loading (2 mg) and separated by cellulose membranes, and the electrochemical performances was tested in 6 M KOH and 1.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution, respectively. The specific capacitance (C, F/g), energy density E (Wh/kg) and power density P (W/kg) of symmetrical supercapacitors are calculated by the following Eqs. (2)–(4) [24].
(2)
C = 4
C
c e l l
= 4 I
Δ
t / m
Δ
V
(3)
E = 1 / 2
C
c e l l
×
Δ
V 2
× 1 / 3.6
(4)
P = E /
Δ
t × 3600
Where Ccell (F/g) denotes the specific capacitance of symmetrical supercapacitor, the rest of the relevant physical quantities are the same as previously described in the three-electrode system.

The photographic images of the YCDs suspension under visible light and UV irradiation are displayed in Fig. 2 a. The transition from yellow to green fluorescence of the YCDs suspension under UV light irradiation is attributed to the π-π* transition of the carbon double bond in YCDs, which is in accordance with the typical characteristics with CDs. The element mapping images (Fig. S1) reveals that O, P, and S are uniformly distributed in the YCDs. The chemical structures of the YCDs, AEC and DAEC are investigated by the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2b). The peaks at 3385 cm−1 and 2871 cm−1 appeared in YCDs are ascribed to the stretching vibration of C-H and O-H [25]. The strong peaks at 1062, 935 cm−1 are related to of P-O and P-C stretching, respectively. The peaks at 1634 and 1532 cm−1 in the AEC spectrum are indexed to the stretching vibrations of C=C/CO and C=N. Major characteristic peaks of YCDs and AEC are all observed in the FTIR spectrum of DAEC. Additionally, in the low magnification TEM image of DAEC (Fig. 2c), monodisperse “black dots” with an average diameter of 3.5 nm uniformly loaded on the surface of the porous carbon. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of DAEC is shown in Fig. 2d, the observed regular stripes with 0.21 nm interlamellar spacing are corresponded to the graphite (100) crystal planes. The above results indicate the successful preparation of porous carbon decorated by 0 D CDs.

Fig. 3 shows the morphology and pore characteristics of EC, AEC, DEC and DAEC. Large number of macroscopic pores are obviously appeared in EC (Fig. 3a), which is attributed to the introduced air bubbles under the continuous whipping of egg whites. While smooth surface without micropores presents in the magnified SEM image (Fig. 3e), indicating that EC provides a reasonable self-template for porous carbon. The interconnected pore structure observed from the SEM images of AEC (Fig. 3b and f) comes from the activation of EC, which provides a large specific surface area for the formation of electrochemical double layer. The SEM images of DEC (Fig. 3c and g) are similar to AEC, presenting a large number of macroscopic pores as well as a surface without micropores. This suggests that the initial morphology of the carbon material is not affected by the introduction of YCDs on the surface. As noticed in Fig. 3d, the macroscopic carbon skeleton of DAEC did not change significantly compared to AEC. Nevertheless, on account of the activization of KOH and the introduction of YCDs, a regular honeycomb-like 3D porous structure can be exhibited in Fig. 3h. The 3D porous electrode with continuous networks enable improve the structural integrity and connectivity for electron transport. In addition, the adequate dispersion of YCDs in porous carbon can fully increase the microporous content and specific surface area as well as promote the penetration and diffusion of electrolyte ions. The EDS elemental mappings (Fig. S2) confirm the uniform distribution of C, O, N, S and P atoms in DAEC, demonstrating the successful introduction of heteroatoms inherited from eggs.

The chemical state and element composition of DAEC have been further determined by XPS survey spectra. As shown in Fig. 4 a, two prominent peaks of C 1 s and O 1 s, three relatively weak N 1 s, S 2p and P 2p peaks are obviously observed from the DAEC spectrum. The deconvolution of C 1 s spectrum of DAEC (Fig. 4b) appears four peaks at the binding energies of 284.8, 285.8, 286.7 and 289.0 eV, corresponding to C=C, C–N/CC, CO, C=O, respectively [26]. The peaks at 530.7, 532.0 and 533.1 eV deconvolved from O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4c) of DAEC imply the presence of C=O, COH /CO-C and -COOH [27]. The peaks at 398.6, 401.3 and 403.2 eV in the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4d) correspond to pyridinic N (N-6), quaternary N (N-Q), and N-oxide (NO), respectively. The existence of N-6 and N-Q can prominently enhance the pseudocapacitance due to the improvement of the electrodes wettability and charge transfer kinetics while the presence of NO facilitates the charge-transfer across interface of electrode-electrolyte. Four peaks at 164.7, 165.8 168.5 and 169.7 eV deconvolved from the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 4e) are related to -C-SOx-C-, C=S, O-S and C-S-C, suggesting the covalent modification of the carbon framework and edges by S atoms, which is conducive to ion storage. As exhibited in Fig. 4f, the peaks of P 2p at 133.2 and 134.8 eV are assigned to P-C and P-O, which is consistent with the FTIR results. Except increasing the specific capacitance, the voltage window of ASC can be broadened due to P-doping suppressing HER and OER in the electrolyte, thus achieving a significant increase in energy density [10–12].

The XRD patterns of EC, AEC, DEC, DAEC are displayed in Fig. 5a. A wide peak at 21.8° and two narrow peaks at 26.4° and 44.5° are observed in all samples, indicating the mainly amorphous phase and partially graphitic structure existed in the prepared materials. Since amorphous carbon facilitates the storage of electrolyte ions and graphitic carbon promotes the rapid transfer of electrons [28–30]. The Raman spectra of all samples (Fig. 5b) exhibit two strong peaks at 1585 cm−1 and 1360 cm−1, which are attributed to the graphitic structure (G band) and the disordered structure (D band), respectively. The ID/IG values of EC, AEC, DEC and DAEC are calculated to be 1.27, 1.23, 1.10 and 1.08. Lower ID/IG value of DAEC compared to others reflects the improvement of graphitization degree, which is beneficial to the transport of charges. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of EC, AEC, DEC and DAEC are displayed in Fig. 5c. The gentle sorption isotherm of EC indicates the non-micropores structure. Compared with EC, both AEC and DAEC present a classical type I/IV adsorption isotherm with a significant H4 hysteresis. The steep increase in N2 adsorption at low relative pressures (P/P0 < 0.1) suggests the existence of abundant micropores. In the moderate relative pressure range (0.4 < P/P0 < 0.9), the apparent capillary condensation step within the hysteresis loop implies the existence of abundant mesopores. While the small vertical tail at high relative pressure (P/P0 > 0.9) proves the presence of few macropores in AEC and DAEC. The pore size distribution of all samples (Fig. 5d) shows that the pores of DAEC are mainly distributed in the range of 0.6-1.8 nm, and these abundant micropores have an important contribution to the expansion of the surface area. The textural parameters of the samples are presented in Table 1 . The largest specific surface area of 1999.3 m2/g and pore volume of 1.22 cm3/g of DAEC compared to EC (3.7 m2/g, 0.005 cm3/g), AEC (1118.3 m2/g, 0.57 cm3/g) and DEC (2.5 m2/g, 0.01 cm3/g). The micropores and mesopores of DAEC provide effective surface area for ions storage, and the mesopores and macropores provide fast pathways for electrolyte ions transport [31,32]. Meanwhile, the large number of micropores suppresses HER in the electrolyte, leading to the expansion of the voltage window. Therefore, DAEC has high specific surface area, abundant micropores, multiple heteroatoms doping and YCDs decoration, which are the properties possessed by electrode materials for high performance ASCs.

The electrochemical performance of EC, AEC, DEC and DAEC was tested in a three-electrode system. The quasi-rectangular CV curves of all electrodes at the scan rates of 100 mV/s (Fig. 6 a) indicate an ideal electrical double-layer capacitive behavior. The CV integrated area of DEC is significantly increased compared to EC, which may be due to the enhanced electrochemical activity of DEC by the abundant functional groups on the surface of YCDs. Compared to the other samples, the largest CV integral area of DAEC signifies the highest charge storage ability, which matches well with its high specific area and the decoration of YCDs. As shown in Fig. 6b, at different scan rates, no severe polarization distortion is observed in the CV curves, manifesting the admirable rate performance of DAEC. It is due to the interconnected porous framework of DAEC, which facilitates adaptation to volume changes during charging/discharging. At a current density of 1 A /g, the GCD curves of all samples (Fig. 6c) appeared as symmetric triangles due to reversible ion adsorption/desorption at the electrode/electrolyte interface The specific capacitance values of EC, AEC, DEC and DAEC are evaluated for 84.7, 196, 239.2 and 472.9 F/g at 1 A/g. The isosceles triangle shapes with small IR drops in the GCD curves at different current densities (Fig. 6d) manifest the outstanding electrochemical reversibility of DAEC. The specific capacitance of DAEC is still 353.3 F/g even at 20 A/g, demonstrating its superior rate capability. The specific capacitance values of EC, AEC, DEC and DAEC are evaluated for 84.7, 196, 239.2 and 472.9 F/g at 1 A/g. The optimal capacitance and rate performance of DAEC suggests that YCDs decoration has a positive effect on the ions and electrons transport. Moreover, the GCD curves of DAEC (Fig. S4) display a little variation after the 5000th cycle, confirming the splendid electrochemical stability.

To insight into the electrochemical kinetics of DAEC, the current contribution from surface-controlled processes and diffusion-controlled processes are separated. The relationship between peak current (i) and scan rate (v) follows the power-law relationship [33].
(5)
i = a
v b
(6)
l o g
i = l o g
a + b l o g
v

Explore the slope of the line between log(i) and log(v) to estimate the values of a and b. The value of b reveals the charge storage mechanism of supercapacitor, either with a dominant diffusion-controlled (b approaches 0.5) or surface-controlled (b approaches 1). From anodic peaks plots of log (i) and log (v) shown in Fig. 6f, it can be observed that the b values of EC, AEC, DEC and DAEC are 0.806, 0.849, 0.946 and 0.946, respectively. The capacitive contribution of DAEC can be analyzed using by separating the current response at a fixed voltage into capacitive (k1 ) and diffusion (k2 ) contribution according the Dunn’s equation [34, 35]:
(7)
i =
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2

Fig. S5 and Fig. 6g present the percentages of capacitive contribution from surface-controlled process (the blue region) and diffusion-controlled processes (the red region) at different scan rates. The surface-controlled capacitive contribution is 63.4%, 65.6%, 68.2%, 78.9% and 82.7% at the scan rates of 5, 10, 20, 50 and 100 mV/s, and surprisingly reach a high value of 98.9% under the scan rates of 200 mV/s, hinting the surface-dominated capacitive response of the DAEC. Therefore, DAEC has an outstanding superior storage capacity and a superior rate ability.

Fig. 6h shows the Nyquist plots for all samples. The intercept of the curve at the real axis and diameter at high frequency region of the semicircle is attributed to the equivalent series resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct). At low frequencies, straight lines nearly perpendicular to the real axis indicate excellent capacitive behavior for all samples. Compared to EC , AEC and DEC, the smaller semicircle diameter and larger linear slope of DAEC demonstrates its excellent conductivity and fast charge/ion transfer rate. Bode plots (Fig. 6i) are further drawn based on EIS analysis. All samples exhibit quite low frequency close to the phase angle of −90◦, which implies their typical capacitive characteristics. The characteristic frequency (f0) at the phase angle of -45° marks the beginning of the transition from capacitive performance to resistive behavior.Compared to the τ0 of EC (4.03 s), AEC (4.86 s) and DEC (4.54 s), the smaller τ0 of DAEC (3.43 s) indicates a more favorable pore structure of DAEC for ion transport [31, 36].

To evaluate the actual practice of DAEC, a symmetric supercapacitor (DAEC//DAEC) was assembled using DAEC as the electrode material and using 6 M KOH as the electrolyte. Fig. 7 a discloses the CV curves of the prepared supercapacitor under various voltage windows with a scan rate of 50 mV/s. The quasi-rectangular CV curve is well maintained under 1.8 V, while showing a slight polarization distortion at 2.0 V, indicating that the assembled symmetric supercapacitor has a superb voltage window of 1.8 V in KOH electrolytes. Moreover, the CV curves of DAEC//DAEC at various scan rates (Fig. 7b) display an ideal quasi-rectangular shape at 1.8 V, which implies ideal electrical double-layer capacitive characteristics. The GCD curves in Fig. S6a remain isosceles triangular shape even at 1.8 V, which is in great agreement with the CV curves. The triangle GCD curves in Fig. 7c without apparent deformation at current densities from 0.5 to 20 A/g demonstrates the excellent reversibility of DAEC//DAEC. The specific capacitance of DAEC//DAEC calculated from the GCD curves is 107.9 F/g at 1 A/g and 85.6 F/g at 20 A/g, revealing the splendid rate performance of DAEC//DAEC.

Compared with alkaline electrolytes, neutral electrolyte such as Na2SO4 have less corrosion and better safety, and the Na+, SO4 2−, trace H+ and OH− in the electrolyte limit the precipitation of hydrogen and oxygen, which can achieve a higher voltage window [23, 24]. Therefore, the electrochemical properties of DAEC//DAEC was further investigated in 1.5 M Na2SO4. As shown in Fig. 7d, the stable quasi-rectangular CV curves in 1.0 ∼ 2.6 V (with a slight anode current increment at 2.8 V) indicates that the voltage window of DAEC//DAEC can be surprisingly extended to 2.6 V in aqueous electrolytes. Furthermore, all the quasi-rectangular CV curves at different scan rates under 2.6 V (Fig. 7e) exhibits the ideal capacitive behaviors of DAEC//DAEC. The GCD curves of DAEC//DAEC at different voltage windows (Fig. S6b) maintain a symmetrical triangle shape even at 2.6 V. These results indicate that the prepared DAEC//DAEC is extremely stable and reversible over an ultra-wide voltage window. The expansion of the voltage window is mainly due to suppression of HER and OER of the electrolyte, which stems from the reversible adsorption of primary hydrogen by the abundant micropores in DAEC, the increased adsorption of cations by the decoration of YCDs and P-doping, and the blocking of electrochemically active oxidation sites by P-functional groups. Additionally, under the voltage window of 2.6 V, the GCD tests were performed at various current densities and the outcomes are presented in Fig. 7f. The standard isosceles triangle shape illustrates the ideal coulombic efficiency of the DAEC//DAEC. The calculated specific capacitances at various current densities are summarized in Table S1. DAEC//DAEC delivers a specific capacitance of 100.6 F/g at 1 A/g and 69.5 F/g at 20 A/g, suggesting its decent rate performance.

The Nyquist plots of DAEC//DAEC in different electrolyte are shown in Fig. 7g. Two small semicircles near the real axis and two nearly vertical lines in the low frequency region. In addition, the values of Rs and Rct of DAEC are 0.46 Ω and 1.19 Ω in KOH electrolyte (1.36 Ω and 1.26 Ω in Na2SO4 electrolyte) manifest the low ohmic resistance and small charge transfer resistance of DAEC//DAEC. The Bode phase diagrams of DAEC//DAEC at different electrolyte are summarized in Fig. S7. The f0 for KOH and Na2SO4 electrolyte at the phase angle of -45° are 0.37 and 0.39 Hz, which corresponds to the τ0 of 2.5 and 2.7 s. Fig. 7h shows the Ragone plots of DAEC//DAEC and other similar work in Table 2 . The maximum energy density of DAEC//DAEC is 51.4 Wh/kg at power density of 450 W/kg and 38.5 Wh/kg preserved at 18,000 W/kg in the KOH electrolyte. In the Na2SO4 electrolyte, an admirable energy density of 107.5 Wh/kg at power density of 650 W/kg and retains 65.2 Wh/kg at 26,000 W/kg, which is dramatically outperforming previously reported ASCs. This is due to the ultrahigh voltage window of DAEC//DAEC played a decisive role in increasing the energy density. The assembled DAEC//DAEC displays splendid cycling stability in Fig. 7i with a capacitance retention of 97.2% and high reversibility with a coulombic efficiency of 100.4% after 5000 GCD cycles at a current density of 10 A/g. The inset shows that two devices connected in series can light up Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs), illustrating the practical application of DAEC. These results suggest that the strategy proposed in this work can effectively widen the voltage window and thus increase the energy density of carbon-based ASCs.

In summary, we fabricated a porous carbon consisting of whipped egg whites foams as a carbon template and yolks-derived CDs for surfactant. The obtained DAEC presents a honeycomb-like 3D interconnected pore structure with a high specific surface area of 1999.3 m2/g, a pore volume of 1.22 cm3/g with abundant micropores and the inherited heteroatoms doping especially P doping. When evaluated in the three electrodes system, the DAEC delivers a high specific capacitance of 472.9 F/g at 1 A/g and a superior capacitance retention of 74.7% even at 20 A/g in KOH electrolyte. In two-electrode system, the voltage window of DAEC//DAEC can be expanded to 1.8 V in KOH and 2.6 V in Na2SO4, respectively. The DAEC//DAEC deliveries an energy density of 51.4 Wh/kg at power density of 250 W/kg and 38.5 Wh/kg in KOH electrolyte, and an extraordinary energy density of 107.5 Wh/kg at a power density of 650 W/kg in Na2SO4 electrolyte. Additionally, the DAEC//DAEC still displays the capacitance retention of 97.2% and coulombic efficiency of 100.4% after 5000 cycles. The design of composite electrode materials for ASCs using the advantageous properties of different components can significantly improve the specific capacitance and voltage window. This work provide insight into the design of carbon-based electrode materials with wide voltage windows and high energy densities for ASCs.

Xin Hou: Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Penggang Ren: Resources, Supervision. Zhong Dai: Conceptualization. Hongtao Chen: Software, Formal analysis. Wenwen Tang: Validation. Zhengyan Chen: Writing – review & editing. Fang Ren: Resources. Yanling Jin: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51773167, 52102303, 52103095), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2019M650268), the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering (Sichuan University) (Grant No. sklpme2021-05-09), and Foundation of Education Department of Shaanxi Province [20JK0805], the authors would like to thank Zhang Yuyao from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the Raman analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2022.140336.
